summary: Nadia reflects on her uncle FM-2030, a futurist and transhumanist who had his body cryonically suspended, and how his seemingly outlandish beliefs about the future now feel prescient. She argues that technological change is exponential rather than linear, citing Kurzweil, meaning the next 15 years will bring unimaginable transformation. This realization leads her to conclude that how we conduct ourselves daily matters enormously. Rather than focusing on grand technological projects, she asks whether our behavior makes society more thoughtful, collaborative, and humane.
key_points: Her uncle FM-2030 was ridiculed for his futurist beliefs, but exponential technological change has vindicated his sense of urgency about the near future
Technological progress is exponential, not linear, meaning past experience is a poor predictor of what comes next
The accelerating pace of change makes existential stakes feel immediate and real rather than science fiction
The most important response to this reality is not building grand technology but conducting oneself with thoughtfulness and humanity in daily life
money_quotes: Nobody could quantify when 'the future' would happen, but the stuff of sci fi novels was obviously light years away.
The older I get, the more I understand that thousands of years of past data cannot predict the future.
It is not farfetched to suggest that we could see the destruction of humankind within our lifetimes. That is not sci fi. That is real.
Suddenly, how we spend our days feels a lot more important.
Does your day-to-day behavior make our society more thoughtful and collaborative, sustain rather than destruct, unite rather than polarize?
Do you, as an individual, affirm our humanity?
key_insight: In an era of exponential change where existential stakes feel immediate, the most meaningful response is not technological ambition but daily conduct that affirms our shared humanity.
